original_abstract: Educational inclusion for students with special needs remains a cornerstone of the global human rights agenda and a defining challenge for modern pedagogical policy. This study provides a comprehensive literature review exploring the barriers and opportunities inherent in implementing inclusive practices, with a specific focus on the Latin American landscape. By synthesizing empirical research, theoretical frameworks, and reports from international organizations, the article identifies critical factors—such as funding, physical infrastructure, teacher training, and institutional coordination—that either hinder or facilitate educational equity. The findings reveal that chronic resource shortages, inadequate professional development, and inaccessible facilities continue to impede progress. The discussion further examines how these systemic obstacles affect both the academic trajectories and socioemotional health of students, argues that inclusion yields dividends for the entire school community rather than just those with disabilities. Ultimately, the paper concludes that achieving true educational equity requires a strategic alignment of local policies, sustained investment in research, and the integration of innovative technologies to support diverse learning needs.
humanized_abstract: Education inclusion of children with disabilities remains a central plank of the global human rights programme and the definitive educational policy issue of our time. Based on a thorough review of the literature on obstacles and resources available for inclusive education, this study focuses specifically on Latin America. Through a synthesis of existing research studies, theories and official reports and investigations from UNESCO, UNICEF and other international organisations the study, drawing on empirical data, analytical perspectives and practical knowledge, investigated the factors which could be obstructive and favourable to the provision of educational opportunities for disadvantaged groups. According to the study, lack of adequate budgets, inefficient use of existing facilities, inadequate teacher training and ineffective coordination of pedagogical structures are only a few of the obstacles faced in the education sector. According to the conclusions of the paper, a sustainable improvement of the educational condition and quality of life of students with disabilities cannot be achieved unless the current barriers, such as lack of financial resources, insufficient teacher training and inadequate facilities are addressed. The study emphasizes that inclusive education is to the benefit of all pupils and schools, and that the key to a fair distribution of education and chances of success is to link local educational policies with research activities combined with the modern technology of learning.
